1	/-
2	Copyright (c) 2026 Christian Merten. All rights reserved.
3	Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
4	Authors: Christian Merten
5	-/
6	import Mathlib
7	import AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.SectionGradedRing
8	import AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.GradedHilbertSerre
9	
10	/-!
11	# The Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf along a fibre (Nitsure §1)
12	
13	This file gives the **real definition** of the Hilbert polynomial
14	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial` of a sheaf of modules `F` on
15	`X ⟶ S` at a point `s : S` relative to a line bundle `L`, via the graded
16	Hilbert function of the fibre section module — the `H⁰`-only encoding pinned
17	in the blueprint (`def:hilbert_polynomial`,
18	`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_QuotScheme.tex`):
19	
20	* the fibre `X_s = π.fiber s` is Mathlib's scheme-theoretic fibre
21	  `pullback π (S.fromSpecResidueField s)`;
22	* the restrictions `F_s`, `L_s` are the module pullbacks along the fibre
23	  embedding `π.fiberι s` (`Scheme.Hom.fiberModule`);
24	* the twists `F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m}` are `Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow` of
25	  `AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.SectionGradedRing`;
26	* their global sections are `κ(s)`-vector spaces via the structural ring
27	  homomorphism `κ(s) →+* Γ(X_s, 𝒪)` (`Scheme.Hom.fiberResidueMap`,
28	  `Scheme.Hom.fiberSectionsModule`), giving the graded Hilbert function
29	  `m ↦ dim_{κ(s)} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})` (`Scheme.hilbertFunction`);
30	* `Scheme.hilbertPolynomial π L F s` is the unique `Φ ∈ ℚ[λ]` that agrees
31	  with the graded Hilbert function for all `m ≫ 0` when such a polynomial
32	  exists, and the junk value `0` otherwise.  Whenever the eventual-match
33	  condition holds the polynomial is unique (two rational polynomials agreeing
34	  at all large naturals are equal), so the classical-choice extraction is
35	  canonical; no numerical invariant is lost (see the Serre-vanishing
36	  discussion in `def:hilbert_polynomial`).
37	
38	The bridge from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality engine is
39	`Scheme.existsUnique_hilbertPolynomial_of_isRatHilb`: when the graded Hilbert
40	function is a rational Hilbert function (`AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb`, the
41	output shape of `AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational`), the
42	eventual-match condition holds, the matching polynomial is unique, and
43	`hilbertPolynomial` computes it (`Scheme.hilbertPolynomial_eq_hilbertPoly`).
44	Producing the `IsRatHilb` hypothesis for proper `X_s` and ample `L_s` is the
45	finite-generation theorem `lem:sectionGradedModule_fg` (Serre), which is
46	future work; it enters only through the lemmas here, never through the
47	definition of `hilbertPolynomial` itself.
48	
49	Blueprint: `def:hilbert_polynomial`, `sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial`
50	(`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_QuotScheme.tex`).
51	Source: [Nitsure], §1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials"
52	(`references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex`, L453–L478);
53	cf. [Hartshorne], III.5.2.
54	-/
55	
56	set_option autoImplicit false
57	
58	universe u
59	
60	open CategoryTheory Limits
61	
62	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
63	
64	namespace Scheme
65	
66	variable {S X : Scheme.{u}}
67	
68	/-- The restriction `F_s = F|_{X_s}` of a sheaf of modules `F` on `X` to the
69	scheme-theoretic fibre `X_s = π.fiber s` of `π : X ⟶ S` over `s : S`: the
70	module pullback along the fibre embedding `π.fiberι s : π.fiber s ⟶ X`.
71	Blueprint: the restriction `F_s` of `def:hilbert_polynomial`. -/
72	noncomputable def Hom.fiberModule (π : X ⟶ S) (s : S) (F : X.Modules) :
73	    (π.fiber s).Modules :=
74	  (Scheme.Modules.pullback (π.fiberι s)).obj F
75	
76	/-- The structural ring homomorphism `κ(s) ⟶ Γ(X_s, 𝒪_{X_s})` of the fibre
77	`X_s = π.fiber s`: global sections of the structural morphism
78	`π.fiberToSpecResidueField s : X_s ⟶ Spec κ(s)`, transported along the
79	canonical isomorphism `Γ(Spec κ(s), ⊤) ≅ κ(s)`.  It makes every section
80	module of a sheaf of modules on the fibre a `κ(s)`-vector space
81	(`Scheme.Hom.fiberSectionsModule`). -/
82	noncomputable def Hom.fiberResidueMap (π : X ⟶ S) (s : S) :
83	    S.residueField s ⟶ Γ(π.fiber s, ⊤) :=
84	  (Scheme.ΓSpecIso (S.residueField s)).inv ≫ (π.fiberToSpecResidueField s).appTop
85	
86	/-- The `κ(s)`-module structure on the global sections of a sheaf of modules
87	`G` on the fibre `X_s = π.fiber s`: restriction of scalars along the
88	structural ring homomorphism `κ(s) →+* Γ(X_s, 𝒪)`
89	(`Scheme.Hom.fiberResidueMap`).  Not an instance (the fibre presentation of
90	the scheme is not canonical); brought into scope with `letI` at use sites. -/
91	@[reducible] noncomputable def Hom.fiberSectionsModule (π : X ⟶ S) (s : S)
92	    (G : (π.fiber s).Modules) :
93	    Module (S.residueField s) Γ(G, ⊤) :=
94	  Module.compHom Γ(G, ⊤) (π.fiberResidueMap s).hom
95	
96	/-- The **graded Hilbert function** of `F` at the fibre over `s : S`, relative
97	to the line bundle `L`:
98	
99	`m ↦ dim_{κ(s)} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})`,
100	
101	the dimension over the residue field `κ(s)` of the global sections of the
102	`m`-th twist of the restriction `F_s` by `L_s` on the scheme-theoretic fibre
103	`X_s = π.fiber s`.  This is the graded Hilbert function of the section graded
104	module `M(X_s, F_s, L_s)` (`def:sectionGradedModule`); it uses only `H⁰`.
105	When the dimension is not finite, `Module.finrank` takes the junk value `0`.
106	Blueprint: the graded Hilbert function of `def:hilbert_polynomial`. -/
107	noncomputable def hilbertFunction (π : X ⟶ S) (L F : X.Modules) (s : S)
108	    (m : ℕ) : ℕ :=
109	  letI := π.fiberSectionsModule s
110	    (Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow (π.fiberModule s F) (π.fiberModule s L) m)
111	  Module.finrank (S.residueField s)
112	    Γ(Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow (π.fiberModule s F) (π.fiberModule s L) m, ⊤)
113	
114	/-- Two rational polynomials that agree at all sufficiently large natural
115	arguments are equal: the agreement set is infinite, so the difference has
116	infinitely many roots. -/
117	private lemma eq_of_eval_nat_eventually_eq {Φ Ψ : Polynomial ℚ} {N₁ N₂ : ℕ}
118	    {f : ℕ → ℚ} (h₁ : ∀ m : ℕ, N₁ < m → Φ.eval (m : ℚ) = f m)
119	    (h₂ : ∀ m : ℕ, N₂ < m → Ψ.eval (m : ℚ) = f m) : Φ = Ψ := by
120	  refine Polynomial.eq_of_infinite_eval_eq _ _ ?_
121	  apply ((Set.Ioi_infinite (max N₁ N₂)).image
122	    (Nat.cast_injective (R := ℚ)).injOn).mono
123	  rintro x ⟨n, hn, rfl⟩
124	  rw [Set.mem_Ioi, sup_lt_iff] at hn
125	  exact Set.mem_setOf_eq ▸ (h₁ n hn.1).trans (h₂ n hn.2).symm
126	
127	open Classical in
128	/-- The **Hilbert polynomial** of a sheaf of modules `F` on `X` over `S` at
129	the fibre over `s ∈ S`, relative to the line bundle `L`
130	(`def:hilbert_polynomial`, [Nitsure] §1; cf. [Hartshorne] III.5.2): the
131	unique polynomial `Φ_{F,s} ∈ ℚ[λ]` that agrees, for all `m ≫ 0`, with the
132	graded Hilbert function
133	
134	`m ↦ dim_{κ(s)} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})`
135	
136	of the fibre section module (`Scheme.hilbertFunction`), when such a
137	polynomial exists — and the junk value `0` otherwise.  Uniqueness holds
138	unconditionally (two polynomials agreeing at all large naturals are equal,
139	`Scheme.hilbertPolynomial_eq_of_eventually`), so the extraction is canonical.
140	
141	For proper `X_s`, ample `L_s` and coherent `F_s` the existence condition
142	holds (graded Hilbert–Serre; the `IsRatHilb`-conditional form is
143	`Scheme.existsUnique_hilbertPolynomial_of_isRatHilb`, and the remaining input
144	is the finite generation `lem:sectionGradedModule_fg`), and for `m ≫ 0` the
145	graded Hilbert function agrees with the Euler characteristic
146	`χ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})` by Serre vanishing, so `Φ_{F,s}` is the classical
147	(Snapper) Hilbert polynomial — no numerical invariant is lost.  When `F` is
148	`S`-flat the function `s ↦ Φ_{F,s}` is locally constant on `S`. -/
149	noncomputable def hilbertPolynomial [IsLocallyNoetherian S] (π : X ⟶ S)
150	    [LocallyOfFiniteType π] (L F : X.Modules) (s : S) :
151	    Polynomial ℚ :=
152	  if h : ∃ Φ : Polynomial ℚ, ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ m : ℕ, N < m →
153	      Φ.eval (m : ℚ) = (hilbertFunction π L F s m : ℚ)
154	  then h.choose else 0
155	
156	section HilbertPolynomialApi
157	
158	variable [IsLocallyNoetherian S] (π : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType π]
159	variable (L F : X.Modules) (s : S)
160	
161	/-- **Well-definedness of the Hilbert polynomial**: any polynomial that agrees
162	with the graded Hilbert function `m ↦ dim_{κ(s)} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})` for
163	all `m ≫ 0` *is* `hilbertPolynomial π L F s`.  This is the uniqueness clause
164	of `def:hilbert_polynomial`. -/
165	theorem hilbertPolynomial_eq_of_eventually {Φ : Polynomial ℚ} {N : ℕ}
166	    (hΦ : ∀ m : ℕ, N < m → Φ.eval (m : ℚ) = (hilbertFunction π L F s m : ℚ)) :
167	    hilbertPolynomial π L F s = Φ := by
168	  have h : ∃ Φ' : Polynomial ℚ, ∃ N' : ℕ, ∀ m : ℕ, N' < m →
169	      Φ'.eval (m : ℚ) = (hilbertFunction π L F s m : ℚ) := ⟨Φ, N, hΦ⟩
170	  rw [hilbertPolynomial, dif_pos h]
171	  obtain ⟨N', hN'⟩ := h.choose_spec
172	  exact eq_of_eval_nat_eventually_eq hN' hΦ
173	
174	/-- **Defining property of the Hilbert polynomial**: if some polynomial
175	agrees with the graded Hilbert function for all `m ≫ 0`, then
176	`hilbertPolynomial π L F s` does. -/
177	theorem hilbertPolynomial_eval_eventually
178	    (h : ∃ Φ : Polynomial ℚ, ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ m : ℕ, N < m →
179	      Φ.eval (m : ℚ) = (hilbertFunction π L F s m : ℚ)) :
180	    ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ m : ℕ, N < m →
181	      (hilbertPolynomial π L F s).eval (m : ℚ) = (hilbertFunction π L F s m : ℚ) := by
182	  obtain ⟨Φ, N, hΦ⟩ := h
183	  refine ⟨N, fun m hm => ?_⟩
184	  rw [hilbertPolynomial_eq_of_eventually π L F s hΦ]
185	  exact hΦ m hm
186	
187	open PowerSeries in
188	omit [IsLocallyNoetherian S] [LocallyOfFiniteType π] in
189	/-- **Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial from rationality of
190	the Hilbert series** (the polynomial-extraction step of
191	`thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule`): if the graded Hilbert function of `F` at
192	`s` is a rational Hilbert function of some order `d`
193	(`AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb`, the output of the graded Hilbert–Serre engine
194	`AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational`), then there is a
195	*unique* polynomial agreeing with it for all `m ≫ 0`.  Combines the
196	rationality datum with Mathlib's `Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly`
197	coefficient extraction (`lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib`). -/
198	theorem existsUnique_hilbertPolynomial_of_isRatHilb {d : ℕ}
199	    (hrat : IsRatHilb (fun m => (hilbertFunction π L F s m : ℚ)) d) :
200	    ∃! Φ : Polynomial ℚ, ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ m : ℕ, N < m →
201	      Φ.eval (m : ℚ) = (hilbertFunction π L F s m : ℚ) := by
202	  obtain ⟨p, N, hp⟩ := hrat
203	  refine ⟨Polynomial.hilbertPoly p d, ⟨max N p.natDegree, fun m hm => ?_⟩, ?_⟩
204	  · rw [← Polynomial.coeff_mul_invOneSubPow_eq_hilbertPoly_eval d
205	      (lt_of_le_of_lt (le_max_right _ _) hm)]
206	    exact (hp m (lt_of_le_of_lt (le_max_left _ _) hm)).symm
207	  · rintro Ψ ⟨N', hΨ⟩
208	    refine eq_of_eval_nat_eventually_eq hΨ
209	      (f := fun m => (hilbertFunction π L F s m : ℚ)) (N₂ := max N p.natDegree)
210	      (fun m hm => ?_)
211	    rw [← Polynomial.coeff_mul_invOneSubPow_eq_hilbertPoly_eval d
212	      (lt_of_le_of_lt (le_max_right _ _) hm)]
213	    exact (hp m (lt_of_le_of_lt (le_max_left _ _) hm)).symm
214	
215	open PowerSeries in
216	/-- Under the rationality hypothesis, `hilbertPolynomial π L F s` satisfies
217	the defining eventual-agreement property with the graded Hilbert function.
218	This is the conditional content of `thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule`; the
219	geometric input producing `IsRatHilb` (properness of `X_s` + ampleness of
220	`L_s` via `lem:sectionGradedModule_fg`) is future work. -/
221	theorem hilbertPolynomial_eval_of_isRatHilb {d : ℕ}
222	    (hrat : IsRatHilb (fun m => (hilbertFunction π L F s m : ℚ)) d) :
223	    ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ m : ℕ, N < m →
224	      (hilbertPolynomial π L F s).eval (m : ℚ) = (hilbertFunction π L F s m : ℚ) :=
225	  hilbertPolynomial_eval_eventually π L F s
226	    (existsUnique_hilbertPolynomial_of_isRatHilb π L F s hrat).exists
227	
228	open PowerSeries in
229	/-- The Hilbert polynomial computes as Mathlib's `Polynomial.hilbertPoly`
230	numerator-extraction applied to any rational-series presentation of the graded
231	Hilbert function. -/
232	theorem hilbertPolynomial_eq_hilbertPoly {p : Polynomial ℚ} {d N : ℕ}
233	    (hp : ∀ m : ℕ, N < m → (hilbertFunction π L F s m : ℚ) =
234	      ((p : ℚ⟦X⟧) * (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ d).val).coeff m) :
235	    hilbertPolynomial π L F s = Polynomial.hilbertPoly p d := by
236	  refine hilbertPolynomial_eq_of_eventually π L F s
237	    (N := max N p.natDegree) (fun m hm => ?_)
238	  rw [← Polynomial.coeff_mul_invOneSubPow_eq_hilbertPoly_eval d
239	    (lt_of_le_of_lt (le_max_right _ _) hm)]
240	  exact (hp m (lt_of_le_of_lt (le_max_left _ _) hm)).symm
241	
242	end HilbertPolynomialApi
243	
244	end Scheme
245	
246	end AlgebraicGeometry

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GradedHilbertSerre.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function
81	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}
139	
140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
160	  % NOTE: Formalization is blocked on the absence of tensor products for sheaves of modules in

160	  % NOTE: Formalization is blocked on the absence of tensor products for sheaves of modules in
161	  % Mathlib at the pinned commit. The degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
162	  % requires a tensor power \(L_s^{\otimes m}\) of a line bundle; this infrastructure (tensor
163	  % product of sheaves of modules with lax-monoidal global sections) is not yet available, making
164	  % this definition a prerequisite sub-build.
165	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
166	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
167	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
168	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
169	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
170	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
171	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
172	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
173	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
174	  %  the function
175	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
176	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
177	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
178	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
179	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
180	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
181	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
182	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
183	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
184	  \[
185	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
186	  \]
187	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
188	  the tensor product of sections
189	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
190	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
191	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
192	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
193	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
194	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
195	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
196	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
197	  polynomial ring).
198	\end{definition}
199	
200	\begin{definition}
201	[Section graded module]
202	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
203	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
204	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, lem:sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
205	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
206	  % Polynomials" (read from
207	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
208	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
209	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
210	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
211	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
212	  %  the function
213	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
214	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
215	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
216	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
217	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
218	  I.7.}
219	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
220	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
221	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
222	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
223	  \[
224	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
225	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
226	  \]
227	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
228	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
229	  by the section tensor product
230	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
231	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
232	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
233	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
234	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
235	\end{definition}
236	
237	\begin{lemma}
238	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
239	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
241	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
242	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
243	  % Polynomials" (read from
244	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
245	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
246	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
247	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
248	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
249	  %  the function
250	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
251	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
252	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
253	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
254	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
255	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
256	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
257	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
258	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
259	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
260	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
261	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
262	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
263	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
264	\end{lemma}
265	
266	\begin{proof}
267	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
268	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
269	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
270	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
271	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
272	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
273	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
274	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
275	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
276	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
277	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
278	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
279	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
280	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
281	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
282	\end{proof}
283	
284	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
285	  graded series]
286	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
287	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
288	  \mathlibok
289	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
290	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
291	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
292	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
293	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
294	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
295	  equals \(h(n)\):
296	  \[
297	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
298	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
299	  \]
300	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
301	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
302	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
303	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
304	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
305	\end{lemma}
306	
307	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
308	  (rank--nullity)]
309	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
310	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
311	  \mathlibok
312	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
313	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
314	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
315	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
316	  \[
317	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
318	  \]
319	  Equivalently, \(\dim_\kappa = \operatorname{finrank}_\kappa\) is additive on a
320	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
321	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
322	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
323	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
324	\end{lemma}
325	
326	\begin{lemma}[The rational power series \((1 - X)^{-d}\)]
327	  \label{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
328	  \lean{PowerSeries.invOneSubPow}
329	  \mathlibok
330	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
331	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PowerSeries.WellKnown}).}
332	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then \((1 - X)^d\) is a
333	  unit in the power series ring \(S\llbracket X\rrbracket\), and Mathlib names its
334	  inverse
335	  \[
336	    \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(S, d) \;=\; (1 - X)^{-d}
337	      \;\in\; S\llbracket X\rrbracket^{\times}.
338	  \]
339	  Together with the coercion
340	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries} : F[X] \to F\llbracket X\rrbracket\) of a
341	  polynomial to its power series, this is the data in which a rational Hilbert
342	  series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) is expressed; it is the same rational-series shape
343	  whose eventual coefficients are extracted by
344	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
345	\end{lemma}
346	
347	\begin{lemma}\leanok
348	[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
349	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
350	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
351	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,
352	    def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
353	    lem:ratHilb_bump, def:graded_subquotientHilb,
354	    lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
355	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
356	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
357	  % 00K1) (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13893-L13905).
358	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
359	  % "Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
360	  %  and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
361	  %  function
362	  %  $$
363	  %  \mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
364	  %  n \longmapsto [M_n]
365	  %  $$
366	  %  see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.
367	  %  If $S_{+}$ is generated by elements of degree $1$,
368	  %  then this function is a numerical polynomial."
369	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", Tag 00K1
370	  (proposition on graded Hilbert polynomials); cf.\ [Nitsure], \S 1 (Snapper's
371	  Lemma), [Hartshorne], I.7.}
372	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
373	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
374	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
375	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
376	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
377	  such that for all \(n > N\)
378	  \[
379	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
380	  \]
381	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
382	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
383	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\), expressed in the power-series form
384	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p) \cdot \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\)
385	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
386	
387	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
388	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
389	  module). It is not covered by Mathlib: \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
390	  supplies only the converse polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not
391	  the production of the rational series from the module. The argument is realised by
392	  an \emph{ambient subquotient induction} (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}):
393	  the whole induction ranges over pairs of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N\) of the
394	  single fixed graded module \(M\), and the Hilbert function is the ambient difference
395	  \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such
396	  pairs is closed under the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one
397	  element (each presented again as an ambient subquotient pair), so the inductive step
398	  never forms a graded quotient ring \(R/(x)\), a graded quotient module \(M/xM\), or a
399	  regrading of a derived carrier. The genuine Mathlib gap is thereby \emph{avoided}
400	  rather than filled; the residual obligation is the short ambient bookkeeping of the
401	  subquotient blocks (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb},
402	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker},
403	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff},
404	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer},
405	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}), each of which manipulates only ambient
406	  families \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\) of submodules of \(M\).
407	  % NOTE: The power-series rationality toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}) is formalized
408	  %   and axiom-clean; the present theorem is a closed, axiom-clean Lean declaration
409	  %   (gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational). The ambient subquotient route
410	  %   (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}) sidesteps missing graded-quotient API by
411	  %   working with ambient families of submodules of \(M\).
412	\end{lemma}
413	
414	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
415	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
416	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
417	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
418	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
419	%  following short exact sequence
420	%  $$
421	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
422	%  $$
423	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
424	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
425	\begin{proof}
426	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
427	    lem:ratHilb_bump, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
428	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
429	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
430	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb,
431	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
432	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
433	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib,
434	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
435	    \leanok
436	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
437	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
438	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
439	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
440	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
441	
442	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
443	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
444	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
445	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
446	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
447	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). The degree-one part
448	  \(R_1\) is then a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space whose \(\kappa\)-basis
449	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) generates \(R\) as a \(\kappa\)-algebra; \(r = \dim_\kappa R_1\)
450	  is the number on which we induct.
451	  % NOTE: The existence of finitely many degree-one generators (the number r the
452	  % induction is on) is expected to follow from the standing hypotheses --
453	  % "finitely generated κ-algebra" together with "generated in degree 1" -- so no
454	  % new hypothesis on gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational is anticipated. To be
455	  % confirmed when the prover formalizes the assembly: degree-1 generation gives
456	  % R₁ as a finite-dimensional κ-vector space spanning R as a κ-algebra, and a
457	  % κ-basis of R₁ is a finite degree-one generating set.
458	
459	  \emph{Set-up of the ambient data.} Regard \(M\) as the fixed ambient graded
460	  \(\kappa\)-module with internal decomposition \(n \mapsto \mathcal{M}_n := M_n\)
461	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}); the components are finite-dimensional. For each
462	  basis vector \(x_i \in R_1\) let \(t_i : M \to M\) be multiplication by \(x_i\), a
463	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism that raises degree by one (it sends \(M_n\) into
464	  \(M_{n+1}\), \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}). Since \(R\) is
465	  commutative the \(t_i\) pairwise commute, and they trivially preserve the pair of
466	  homogeneous submodules \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\). Because \(R\) is generated by
467	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) and \(M\) is finitely generated over \(R\), \(M\) is finitely
468	  generated as a module over the polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) acting
469	  through the \(t_i\); this is the finiteness condition required below.
470	
471	  \emph{Invoking the ambient subquotient induction.} Apply
472	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} to the pair \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\)
473	  with the \(r\) commuting degree-\((+1)\) endomorphisms \(t_1, \dots, t_r\). It
474	  yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) of order \(r\) for the ambient subquotient Hilbert
475	  function of this pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}),
476	  \[
477	    h(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(\top \cap \mathcal{M}_n) - \dim_\kappa(\bot \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
478	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n - 0 \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n .
479	  \]
480	  Hence \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa M_n,\, r)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}):
481	  there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d := r \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
482	  with
483	  \[
484	    \dim_\kappa M_n \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)
485	    \qquad (n > N),
486	  \]
487	  which is the claim, with pole order \(d = r\). The internal mechanics of
488	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} -- the base case via
489	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}, and the inductive step assembling the
490	  kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}), the degreewise
491	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, built on
492	  \(D_5\), \cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) and the finiteness transfer
493	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) into
494	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq} and \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump} -- are the Stacks~00K1
495	  induction realised entirely with ambient homogeneous submodules of \(M\).
496	\end{proof}
497	
498	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
499	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}

data.content:
## Status (iter-176 Lane H file-skeleton — re-dispatch)

iter-175 Lane H died to the Anthropic session-limit reset window without
ever calling `Write` (the file was never created). iter-176 re-dispatches
the file-skeleton verbatim. Each of the six blueprint-pinned declarations
carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching the
`\lean{...}` pin in `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_QuotScheme.tex`) with a
`sorry` body. The bodies are iter-177+ work; the substantive proofs are
deep (Nitsure §5: boundedness ⟶ Grassmannian embedding ⟶ flattening
stratification ⟶ valuative criterion).

The 6 blueprint-pinned declarations are:

500	% NOTE: Declarations in this subsection are currently \texttt{private} in the Lean
501	% source; \lean{} pins may not resolve until they are moved to a dedicated module.
502	
503	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
504	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
505	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
506	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
507	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
508	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
509	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
510	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
511	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
512	
513	\begin{lemma}\leanok
514	[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
515	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
516	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
517	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
518	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
519	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
520	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
521	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
522	  \(F\):
523	  \[
524	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
525	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
526	  \]
527	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
528	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
529	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
530	  the Cauchy product.
531	\end{lemma}
532	
533	\begin{lemma}\leanok
534	[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
535	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
536	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
537	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
538	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
539	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
540	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
541	  \[
542	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
543	  \]
544	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
545	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
546	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
547	  \[
548	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
549	  \]
550	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
551	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
552	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
553	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
554	\end{lemma}
555	
556	\begin{proof}
557	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
558	  \leanok
559	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
560	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
561	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
562	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
563	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
564	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
565	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
566	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
567	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
568	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
569	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
570	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
571	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
572	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
573	\end{proof}
574	
575	\begin{definition}\leanok
576	[Rational Hilbert function]
577	  \label{def:ratHilb}
578	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
579	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
580	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
581	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
582	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
583	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
584	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
585	  \[
586	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
587	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
588	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
589	  \]
590	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
591	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
592	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
593	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
594	\end{definition}
595	
596	\begin{lemma}\leanok
597	[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
598	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
599	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
600	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
601	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
602	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
603	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
604	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
605	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
606	\end{lemma}
607	
608	\begin{lemma}\leanok
609	[Raising the pole order]
610	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
611	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
612	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
613	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
614	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
615	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
616	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
617	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
618	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
619	\end{lemma}
620	
621	\begin{lemma}\leanok
622	[Closure under difference]
623	  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
624	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
625	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
626	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
627	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
628	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
629	  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
630	  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
631	\end{lemma}
632	
633	\begin{lemma}\leanok
634	[Closure under right shift]
635	  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
636	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
637	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
638	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
639	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient
640	  sequences is multiplication of the series by \(X\), so the numerator
641	  \(p\) is replaced by \(X\cdot p\) at the same pole order. Proved directly in
642	  Lean.
643	\end{lemma}
644	
645	\begin{lemma}\leanok
646	[Antidifference step for the predicate]
647	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
648	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
649	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
650	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
651	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
652	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
653	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
654	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
655	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
656	\end{lemma}
657	
658	\begin{proof}
659	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
660	  \leanok
661	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
662	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
663	  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
664	  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
665	  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
666	  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
667	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
668	\end{proof}
669	
670	\begin{lemma}\leanok
671	[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
672	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
673	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
674	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
675	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
676	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
677	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
678	  \[
679	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
680	  \]
681	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
682	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
683	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
684	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
685	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
686	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
687	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
688	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
689	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
690	\end{lemma}
691	
692	\begin{proof}
693	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
694	  \leanok
695	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
696	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
697	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
698	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
699	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
700	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
701	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
702	\end{proof}
703	
704	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
705	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
706	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
707	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
708	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
709	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
710	  % Polynomials" (read from
711	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
712	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
713	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
714	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
715	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
716	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
717	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
718	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
719	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
720	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
721	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
722	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
723	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
724	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
725	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
726	  \[
727	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
728	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
729	  \]
730	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
731	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
732	\end{theorem}
733	
734	\begin{proof}
735	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
736	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
737	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
738	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
739	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
740	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
741	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
742	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
743	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
744	  function
745	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
746	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
747	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
748	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
749	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
750	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
751	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
752	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
753	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
754	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
755	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
756	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
757	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
758	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
759	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
760	\end{proof}
761	
762	\subsection{The ambient subquotient induction for the Stacks~00K1 step}
763	\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}
764	
765	The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
766	in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
767	the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction. Rather than build the
768	kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one element as graded modules
769	over a smaller quotient ring \(R/(x)\) -- which would force a graded structure on a
770	derived carrier -- we range the induction over \emph{pairs of homogeneous
771	submodules of a single fixed ambient graded module} \(M\). A pair \((N, N')\) with
772	\(N' \le N\) homogeneous submodules of \(M\) stands for the subquotient \(N/N'\); its
773	ambient Hilbert function is the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) -
774	\dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such pairs, equipped with finitely many
775	pairwise-commuting degree-one endomorphisms preserving \(N\) and \(N'\), is
776	\emph{closed} under the kernel and cokernel of one of those endomorphisms, both
777	again presented as ambient subquotient pairs. Consequently no graded quotient ring,
778	graded quotient module, or regrading of a subtype/quotient carrier is ever formed:
779	every object that appears is an ambient family \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\)
780	of submodules of the fixed \(M\).
781	
782	This subsection records that machinery as a thin layer over existing Mathlib
783	scaffold: Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
784	(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of
785	homogeneous elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the degree-shift
786	of multiplication by a homogeneous element
787	(\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
788	(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
789	transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
790	What is supplied here is the internal direct-sum decomposition of a homogeneous
791	submodule as an ambient family (the split \(G_1\)), the subquotient Hilbert data,
792	the kernel/cokernel closure, the degreewise difference identity, the ambient
793	finiteness transfer, and the induction itself.
794	
795	\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
796	ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
797	\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a graded
798	\(\kappa\)-vector space (the fixed ambient module) with finite-dimensional
799	components \(M_n\). All submodules \(N, N', \dots\) below are homogeneous submodules
800	of \(M\), and the relevant endomorphisms are \(\kappa\)-linear maps \(M \to M\)
801	raising degree by one -- typically multiplication by a homogeneous element
802	\(x \in R_1\).
803	
804	\subsubsection*{Mathlib dependency anchors}
805	
806	\begin{lemma}[Homogeneous submodule]
807	  \label{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
808	  \lean{Submodule.IsHomogeneous}
809	  \mathlibok
810	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
811	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Submodule}).}
812	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) an
813	  internally graded \(R\)-module. A submodule \(p \subseteq M\) is
814	  \emph{homogeneous} when it is closed under taking homogeneous components:
815	  \(m \in p\) implies each graded piece \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\). Equivalently
816	  \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\) as a set. Mathlib bundles this into a homogeneous
817	  submodule type providing order and extensionality structure; the ambient internal
818	  direct-sum decomposition \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\) is supplied by the split
819	  \(G_1\) (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
820	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}).
821	\end{lemma}
822	
823	\begin{lemma}[A span of homogeneous elements is homogeneous]
824	  \label{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}
825	  \lean{Ideal.homogeneous_span}
826	  \mathlibok
827	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
828	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Ideal}).}
829	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(s \subseteq R\) a set each of
830	  whose elements is homogeneous. Then the ideal \(\mathrm{span}\,s\) is a
831	  homogeneous ideal. In particular, for \(x \in R_1\) the principal ideal \((x)\)
832	  is homogeneous, hence \(xM\) and the auxiliary submodule \(N' + x \cdot N\) are
833	  homogeneous; this homogeneity feeds the kernel/cokernel closure
834	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
835	\end{lemma}
836	
837	\begin{lemma}[Rank--nullity]
838	  \label{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}
839	  \lean{LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker}

## Status (iter-176 Lane H file-skeleton — re-dispatch; §1 closed run 0011)

iter-175 Lane H died to the Anthropic session-limit reset window without
ever calling `Write` (the file was never created). iter-176 re-dispatches
the file-skeleton verbatim. Each blueprint-pinned declaration carries the
*intended* substantive type signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_QuotScheme.tex`); declaration 1
(`hilbertPolynomial`) is now a **real definition** imported from
`AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.HilbertPolynomial` (run 0011), while the bodies
of the remaining headline declarations are still typed `sorry`s — the
substantive proofs are deep (Nitsure §5: boundedness ⟶ Grassmannian
embedding ⟶ flattening stratification ⟶ valuative criterion).

The 6 blueprint-pinned declarations are:

1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial` (def) — the
   **Hilbert polynomial function** `s ↦ Φ_{F,s} ∈ ℚ[λ]` of a coherent
   sheaf `F` on `X` over a finite-type `π : X ⟶ S` with respect to a
   line bundle `L`. **Now a real definition** — provided (sorry-free,
   with its uniqueness/spec API) by
   `AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.HilbertPolynomial`; see §1 below.

2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor` (def, ~6 LOC) — the **Quot
   functor** `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S} : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` sending an
   `S`-scheme `T ⟶ S` to the set of equivalence classes
   `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` with `F` a `T`-flat coherent sheaf on
   `X_T`, `q : E_T ↠ F` a surjection, and `F|_{X_t}` having Hilbert
   polynomial `Φ` at every `t ∈ T`.

3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian` (def, ~5 LOC) — the
   **Grassmannian functor** `Grass(V, d) : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` of
   rank-`d` quotients of a locally free `O_S`-module `V`.

4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian.representable` (theorem, ~8 LOC)
   — the **representability of the Grassmannian** by a smooth projective
   `S`-scheme `Gr_S(V, d)` of relative dimension `d(r-d)`, equipped with
   the Plücker closed embedding into `ℙ_S(⋀^d V)`.

5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the
   **Grothendieck–Altman–Kleiman representability theorem** for the Quot
   functor: for a noetherian `S`, a projective `π : X ⟶ S`, a relatively
   very ample `L` on `X`, a coherent `E`, and `Φ ∈ ℚ[λ]`, the functor
   `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` is representable by a projective `S`-scheme.

6. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChangeCohomology` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the
   **flat base-change theorem of cohomology** (Stacks tag 02KH): for a
   cartesian square with `g` flat and `f` quasi-compact quasi-separated,
   the canonical base-change map `g* (f_* F) ⟶ f'_* ((g')* F)` is an
   isomorphism. The current scaffold encodes the `i = 0` direct-image
   form (substantive content of (ii) of the Stacks 02KH statement); the
   `R^i f_*` form for `i ≥ 1` requires the higher-direct-image
   infrastructure not present at the pinned commit.

## Note on type expressivity

Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
each declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:

- `hilbertPolynomial` returns `Polynomial ℚ` keyed by `s : S`, not
  `Unit`; the Hilbert polynomial is a non-trivial invariant of the
  coherent sheaf at the fiber over `s`.
- `QuotFunctor` and `Grassmannian` return contravariant functors into
  `Type u` — substantive presheaves of sets, not constant functors.
- `Grassmannian.representable` and `QuotScheme` package the
  `Functor.RepresentableBy` Yoneda-bijection structure: existence of a
  scheme `Y` together with a `RepresentableBy Y` witness — substantive
  content (a representable functor is determined by its representing
  object up to canonical isomorphism, and the witness is the data of
  that isomorphism family).
- `flatBaseChangeCohomology` produces a `Nonempty (... ≅ ...)` of an
  isomorphism between two `S'`-modules built via the pullback/pushforward
  bifunctor; the iso is non-trivial (it is `Stacks 02KH` content, not
  the identity-on-the-same-object iso `Iso.refl _`).

## Mathlib status

Mathlib (master `b80f227`) provides:
- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules` (the category `X.Modules`),
- `Scheme.Modules.pullback`, `Scheme.Modules.pushforward` (the
  pullback–pushforward adjunction at level `i = 0`),
- `CategoryTheory.IsPullback` for cartesian squares,
- `CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy` for representable functors,
- `AlgebraicGeometry.Flat`, `AlgebraicGeometry.QuasiCompact`,
  `AlgebraicGeometry.QuasiSeparated`, `AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper`,
  `AlgebraicGeometry.LocallyOfFiniteType`, `AlgebraicGeometry.IsLocallyNoetherian`
  (morphism / object property predicates), and
- `Polynomial` for `ℚ[λ]`.

Mathlib does NOT provide (at the pinned commit):
- a Grassmannian *scheme* (only a linear-algebra Grassmannian
  as a finite-rank-quotient variety),
- a `IsProjective` morphism property,
- the Quot/Hilbert functor or its representability,
- `R^i f_*` higher direct images on `Scheme.Modules`,
- Castelnuovo–Mumford `m`-regularity,
- Snapper's Lemma for the polynomial property of Euler characteristics.

The current file-skeleton uses `IsProper π` as the structural stand-in
for "projective `π`" (every projective morphism is proper; the

input.description: Read Nitsure section 1 stratification passage L440-500

data.content:
\medskip


\bigskip

\centerline{\large\bf Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials}

\medskip


Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on 
$X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
the function 
$${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) = 
\sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the 
coherence and properness conditions. The fact that $\chi(F(m))$
is indeed a polynomial in $m$ under the above assumption is a special
case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).



Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
be any coherent sheaf on 
$X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$ 
which is the Hilbert polynomial of
the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$. 
If $\F$ is flat over $S$ then the function $s\mapsto {\Phi}_s$ 
from the set of points of $S$ to the polynomial ring $\Q[\lambda]$ is 
known to be locally constant on $S$. 


This shows that the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}$ naturally decomposes
as a co-product 
$$\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$$
where for any polynomial ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$, the functor 
$\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ associates
to any $T$ the set of all equivalence classes of 
families $\langle \F,q \rangle$ such that at each $t\in T$ the 
Hilbert polynomial of the restriction $\F_t$, calculated
using the pull-back of $L$, is ${\Phi}$. Correspondingly,
the representing scheme  $\quot_{E/X/S}$, when it exists, naturally decomposes
as a co-product
$$\quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$$

\textbf{Note } 
We will generally take $X$ to be (quasi-)projective over $S$,
and $L$ to be a relatively very ample line bundle. Then indeed 
the Hilbert and Quot functors are representable by schemes,
but not in general.

127:noncomputable def moduleTensorPow (F L : X.Modules) (m : ℕ) : X.Modules :=
---
  (\cref{lem:braiding_eq_id_of_invertible}) by a basis-local argument; in
  particular \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{L})\) itself need not be an invertible
  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-module. This is the line-bundle hypothesis under which
  the section graded ring becomes commutative
  (\cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}). The structure sheaf \(\mathbf{1}_X\)
  is invertible (it trivializes on the basis of all opens), and any tensor power
  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) of an invertible sheaf is again invertible.
\end{definition}


\begin{definition}\leanok
[Twist of a coherent sheaf by a tensor power]
  \label{def:sheafModuleTwist}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow}
  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:sheafTensorPow}
  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
  a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\)) and \(\mathcal{L}\) a line bundle. For
  \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the \emph{\(m\)-twist} of \(\mathcal{F}\) by
  \(\mathcal{L}\) is
  \[
    \mathcal{F}(m) \;:=\;
      \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}
      \qquad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}),
  \]
  the degree-\(m\) carrier of the section graded module. By construction
  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{O}_X \cong
  \mathcal{F}\).
\end{definition}


The next four isomorphisms are the parts of the (would-be) symmetric monoidal
structure on \(X.\Modules\) that descend through sheafification from
\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} using only \emph{functoriality} of the
sheafification functor (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) and, for the
unitors, the reflective counit isomorphism --- no strong monoidality of
sheafification is required. They are the structural inputs of the tensor-power
comparison \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.


The strong-monoidality comparison \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
below is assembled from three reduction lemmas --- a localization criterion, the
local-isomorphism property of the unit, and their corollary --- together with the
coequalizer presentation of the relative module-presheaf tensor product, the single
Mathlib-absent brick on which the comparison rests.


\begin{definition}

[Scheme-level module sheafification functor]

5276	\section{Lean encoding}
5277	\label{sec:quot_lean_encoding}
5278	
5279	The Lean target file is the new
5280	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean}. The construction
5281	proceeds in three phases.
5282	
5283	\paragraph{Phase 1: Hilbert polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}).}
5284	On a finite-type morphism \(\pi : X \to S\) with \(S\) noetherian and a
5285	coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) with proper support over \(S\), the per-fiber
5286	Hilbert polynomial is encoded as a function \(s \mapsto
5287	\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). Mathlib provides
5288	\texttt{CategoryTheory.PolynomialModule} and Euler-characteristic
5289	infrastructure on coherent sheaves on noetherian schemes; the polynomial
5290	property of \(m \mapsto \chi(\mathcal{F} \otimes L^{\otimes m})\) requires
5291	Snapper's Lemma, which is a project-side sub-build on top of the
5292	graded-Euler-characteristic infrastructure.
5293	
5294	\paragraph{Phase 2: Grassmannian scheme (\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme},
5295	\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).} The Grassmannian
5296	\(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) is encoded as the relative gluing of \(\binom{r}{d}\)
5297	affine charts \(U^I\) (each isomorphic to a relative affine space
5298	\(\mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_S\)) via the Pl\"ucker cocycle \(\theta_{I,J}\). The
5299	gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData}. The
5300	universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\) is glued by the transition cocycle
5301	\(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), and the determinant line bundle
5302	\(\det \mathcal{U}\) gives the Pl\"ucker closed embedding into
5303	\(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\). The representability statement uses
5304	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} (as in
5305	\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) on the universal pair
5306	\((\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d), \mathcal{U})\).
5307	
5308	\paragraph{Phase 3: Quot scheme (\cref{thm:quot_representable}).}
5309	The Quot functor is constructed as a locally closed subfunctor of the
5310	Grassmannian
5311	\(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\), via:
5312	\begin{enumerate}
5313	  \item the uniform \(m\)-regularity bound from
5314	    \cref{lem:quot_boundedness} (input: Castelnuovo--Mumford theorem,
5315	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.CastelnuovoMumford} -- project-side sub-build);
5316	  \item the Grassmannian embedding \(\alpha\) from
5317	    \cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective};
5318	  \item the flattening stratification (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
5319	    of \cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}, A.2.a) applied to the
5320	    universal quotient on the Grassmannian, producing the locally closed
5321	    stratum \(T_0^\Phi\);
5322	  \item the valuative-criterion upgrade
5323	    (\cref{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}) to closed embedding, hence
5324	    projective \(S\)-scheme.
5325	\end{enumerate}
5326	The reduction \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W} from the universal
5327	case to the general case is encoded as a closed embedding of representable
5328	subfunctors via the kernel-vanishing scheme.
5329	
5330	\paragraph{Cohomology and base change.}
5331	\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology} is the Mathlib-equivalent of
5332	Stacks tag 02KH. Mathlib carries a partial form
5333	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.flat\_base\_change}) for affine bases; the
5334	full \(R^i f_*\) statement is a project-side bridge if not present at the
5335	pinned commit.
5336	
5337	\paragraph{Dependencies summary.}
5338	This file directly consumes
5339	\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (A.1.a, on disk) for relative-spectrum
5340	gluing infrastructure and
5341	\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists} from
5342	\cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification} (A.2.a, written this iter)
5343	for the flattening stratum. Downstream, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}
5344	(A.2.c) consumes \cref{thm:quot_representable} via the special case
5345	\(E = \mathcal{O}_C\) on a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), giving the Hilbert
5346	scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot^{\Phi,\mathcal{O}_C(1)}_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
5347	and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective divisors.
5348	
5349	\section{Out of scope}
5350	\label{sec:quot_out_of_scope}
5351	
5352	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Quot-foundations layer: the Hilbert
5353	polynomial, the Quot functor, and the Grassmannian scheme with its
5354	representability. The following are explicitly out of scope here:
5355	\begin{itemize}
5356	  \item \textbf{Representability of the Quot scheme} -- the
5357	    Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman existence theorem and its proof skeleton
5358	    (boundedness, Grassmannian embedding, flattening stratification,
5359	    valuative criterion) are not constructed in this chapter.
5360	  \item \textbf{Flat base change of cohomology} (Stacks 02KH) and its
5361	    associated prerequisites -- not constructed in this chapter.
5362	  \item \textbf{Hilbert scheme as a separate chapter}. The Hilbert scheme
5363	    \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) is the special case
5364	    \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\) of the Quot functor and is
5365	    introduced inline (\cref{def:quot_functor}); no separate chapter.
5366	  \item \textbf{Snapper's Lemma} -- the polynomial-eventually property of
5367	    Euler characteristics, used in \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Cited
5368	    via Nitsure \S 1 and (textbook reference) Hartshorne III.5.2;
5369	    Lean formalization is a prerequisite sub-task.
5370	  \item \textbf{Identification with \(\mathbb{P}^n\) and relative
5371	    Grassmannian variants} (Nitsure \S 1, Exercises (1)--(4)) --
5372	    optional applications, not consumed by Route A.
5373	  \item \textbf{Group-scheme structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)} (A.2.c) -- the
5374	    abelian-group-scheme refinement is downstream. Its blueprint home is the
5375	    FGA Picard-representability assembly (the chapter that bundles the relative
5376	    Picard functor, its representability, and the abelian-group-scheme
5377	    structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)); that assembly lives in the parent project
5378	    which consumes this Quot-foundations layer and is not part of the present
5379	    subproject.
5380	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety
5381	    structure} (A.3) -- a separate Route A sub-row, fed by but not
5382	    constructed in this chapter.
5383	\end{itemize}
