4158	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^* \mathcal{F}\). For every \(i \ge 0\):
4159	  \begin{enumerate}
4160	    \item the base-change map
4161	      \(g^* R^i f_* \mathcal{F} \to R^i f'_* \mathcal{F}'\) is an isomorphism;
4162	    \item if \(S = \Spec A\) and \(S' = \Spec B\), then \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F})
4163	      \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\).
4164	  \end{enumerate}
4165	\end{theorem}
4166	
4167	\subsection{Project-side base-change substrate}
4168	\label{subsec:quot_project_basechange}
4169	
4170	The Lean encoding of \cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology} (and the
4171	Quot construction's iso between pushforward and tensor-pullback for the
4172	universal section pattern) relies on four project-side declarations
4173	introduced in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean}. These
4174	are typed-sorry today: each carries a substantive Lean type that
4175	encodes the intended mathematical statement, with the body deferred to
4176	later iters (see \cref{sec:quot_lean_encoding} for the
4177	phase-by-phase plan).
4178	
4179	\begin{definition}\leanok
4180	[Canonical base-change map on sheaves of modules]
4181	  \label{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map}
4182	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap}
4183	  \uses{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
4184	  Given a cartesian square of schemes
4185	  \(\square = (g' : X' \to X,\ f' : X' \to S',\ f : X \to S,\ g : S' \to S)\)
4186	  with \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) and \(g\) flat, the
4187	  \emph{canonical base-change map} on sheaves of modules is the natural
4188	  transformation \(g'^* f^* \mathcal{F} \longrightarrow f'^* g^* \mathcal{F}\)
4189	  arising from the universal property of pullback. Encoded as a morphism
4190	  of \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\)-valued sheaf functors on the pulled-back open
4191	  cover; this is the morphism whose iso-status discharges
4192	  \cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}(1) in the present
4193	  finite-locally-free / quasi-coherent-of-finite-type setting.
4194	\end{definition}

This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.

input.new_string:
This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. It first records how a fibre \(X_s\) of a
family \(\pi : X \to S\) carries the data the construction consumes: the
restrictions \(\mathcal{F}_s\) and \(L_s\)
(\cref{def:fiber_module_restriction}) and the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector-space
structure on their sections (\cref{def:fiber_sections_module}). Working over
the fibre \(X_s\) with its ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf
\(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the section graded ring
(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule})
together with the graded Hilbert function
(\cref{def:fiber_hilbert_function}), records their finite generation
(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}): the graded Hilbert--Serre
rationality of the Hilbert series (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational})
combines with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction lemma
(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}) in \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_of_isRatHilb},
and the extracted polynomial is unique by
\cref{lem:hilbertPolynomial_unique}. The route uses only \(H^0\)
(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.

\begin{definition}\leanok
[Restriction of a sheaf of modules to a fibre]
  \label{def:fiber_module_restriction}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.fiberModule}
  \source{nitsure-hilbert-quot}
  % ENCODING. The fibre X_s is Mathlib's scheme-theoretic fibre
  % π.fiber s = pullback π (S.fromSpecResidueField s) = X ×_S Spec κ(s), with
  % fibre embedding π.fiberι s : π.fiber s ⟶ X; the restriction is the module
  % pullback (Scheme.Modules.pullback (π.fiberι s)).obj F. Formalized
  % sorry-free in AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/HilbertPolynomial.lean.
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
  Let \(\pi : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(s \in S\) a point with
  residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{fibre} of \(\pi\) over \(s\) is the
  scheme
  \[
    X_s \;=\; X \times_S \Spec \kappa(s),
  \]
  the base change of \(\pi\) along the canonical morphism
  \(\Spec \kappa(s) \to S\); write \(\iota_s : X_s \to X\) for the projection
  to \(X\) (the \emph{fibre embedding}). For a sheaf of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\), the \emph{restriction of
  \(\mathcal{F}\) to the fibre} is the module pullback
  \[
    \mathcal{F}_s \;=\; \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \;:=\; \iota_s^{*} \mathcal{F},
  \]
  a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{X_s}\)-modules. In particular a line bundle \(L\)
  on \(X\) restricts to a line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\) on \(X_s\).
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}\leanok
[\(\kappa(s)\)-structure on sections over a fibre]
  \label{def:fiber_sections_module}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.fiberResidueMap,
    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.fiberSectionsModule}
  \uses{def:fiber_module_restriction}
  \source{nitsure-hilbert-quot}
  % ENCODING. One mathematical construction, two Lean declarations:
  % Scheme.Hom.fiberResidueMap π s : S.residueField s ⟶ Γ(π.fiber s, ⊤) is
  % Γ of the structural morphism π.fiberToSpecResidueField s transported along
  % ΓSpecIso, and Scheme.Hom.fiberSectionsModule π s G is the resulting
  % Module (S.residueField s) Γ(G, ⊤) by restriction of scalars
  % (Module.compHom). Formalized sorry-free in
  % AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/HilbertPolynomial.lean.
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
  Let \(\pi : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(s \in S\). The fibre
  \(X_s = X \times_S \Spec \kappa(s)\) (\cref{def:fiber_module_restriction})
  carries a structural morphism \(p_s : X_s \to \Spec \kappa(s)\), the second
  projection of the fibre product. Taking global sections of \(p_s\) and
  identifying \(\Gamma(\Spec \kappa(s), \mathcal{O}) = \kappa(s)\) yields the
  \emph{structural ring homomorphism of the fibre}
  \[
    \kappa(s) \;\longrightarrow\; \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{O}_{X_s}\bigr).
  \]
  For any sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{X_s}\)-modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on the fibre,
  the global sections \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{G})\) form a module over
  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), hence, by restriction of scalars along
  the structural homomorphism, a \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space. All dimensions
  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)}\) of section spaces over a fibre are taken with respect
  to this \(\kappa(s)\)-structure.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}\leanok
[Graded Hilbert function along a fibre]
  \label{def:fiber_hilbert_function}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertFunction}
  \uses{def:fiber_module_restriction, def:fiber_sections_module,
    def:sheafModuleTwist}
  \source{nitsure-hilbert-quot}
  % ENCODING. Scheme.hilbertFunction π L F s m is
  % Module.finrank (S.residueField s)
  % Γ(Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow (π.fiberModule s F) (π.fiberModule s L) m, ⊤)
  % with the κ(s)-module structure brought into scope by
  % π.fiberSectionsModule s (def:fiber_sections_module); the twist is
  % def:sheafModuleTwist of chapter Picard_SectionGradedRing applied on the
  % fibre. Module.finrank returns the junk value 0 when the dimension is not
  % finite, so the Lean function is total. Formalized sorry-free in
  % AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/HilbertPolynomial.lean.
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
  Let \(S\) be a locally noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a morphism
  locally of finite type, \(L\) a line bundle and \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules on \(X\), and \(s \in S\). The \emph{graded
  Hilbert function of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\) relative to \(L\)} is the
  function
  \[
    h_{\mathcal{F},s} : \mathbb{N} \longrightarrow \mathbb{N}, \qquad
    h_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) \;=\;
    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
  \]
  where \(\mathcal{F}_s\) and \(L_s\) are the restrictions to the fibre
  \(X_s\) (\cref{def:fiber_module_restriction}), the twist
  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) is the \(m\)-twist of
  \(\mathcal{F}_s\) by \(L_s\) on \(X_s\) (\cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}), and
  the dimension is taken with respect to the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector-space
  structure on sections over the fibre (\cref{def:fiber_sections_module}).
  This is exactly the graded Hilbert function of the section graded module
  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}); it uses
  only global sections \(H^0\).
\end{definition}

---
name: horizon-commit
description: Record your own work in the workspace ledger with semantic commits via `horizon commit` — small, meaningful, math-first messages; safe under concurrent runs.
---

Commit your work yourself, semantically, as you finish coherent pieces — don't
leave one giant undifferentiated snapshot for the system to sweep up. Good
commits are how the dashboard's change view and a human reviewer understand what
each session actually did.

## How

Use the Horizon CLI (never raw `git` on the ledger — the workspace repo is
out-of-tree with excludes, a secret guard, and provenance trailers that a bare
`git` would bypass):

- `"$HORIZON_BIN" commit -m "<message>" <files…>` — commit the files you changed.
  Paths are relative to your current directory (your project dir) or absolute.
- `"$HORIZON_BIN" commit -m "<message>" --changed` — commit everything you
  changed in the project (excludes the shared Horizon state, which the system
  commits separately).

Provenance (run / session / role / task / projects) is stamped automatically —
do **not** pass it. The commit is attributed to your role.

## Messages — math first

Write the message the way you'd tell a mathematician what you did: the result
and the idea, in human-readable prose with LaTeX where it helps; mention Lean
declaration names / files only as supporting detail. Mirror the house style,
e.g. `Prove H^i(X,F)=0 for i>dim X via the Čech-to-derived comparison`. One
coherent step per commit; split unrelated work into separate commits.

## Concurrency — you are not alone

Other runs may be committing to the **same** ledger at the same time. That's
fine and intended (it helps everyone converge faster):

- Commits are a single shared branch — they serialise and never conflict as a
  git *merge*; if `horizon commit` hits contention it already retried, so trust
  its result.
- Commit only the files **you** changed. On a shared worktree your commit can
  still capture another run's in-flight edit to the same file — that's expected
  and acceptable; the message + trailers still record your intent.
- Never `reset`, `rebase`, force-push, or otherwise rewrite the ledger. `horizon
  commit` only ever appends a commit.

## When

Commit at each coherent milestone (a lemma closed, a file completed), not only
at the end. Anything you don't commit is still captured by the system's
end-of-session sweep, but as an unlabelled blob — so commit it yourself to keep
the history legible.

\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
  % Polynomials" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
  %  be any coherent sheaf on
  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
  \[
    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
  \]
  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
  function
  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
\end{proof}
input.new_string:
\begin{lemma}\leanok
[Uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial]
  \label{lem:hilbertPolynomial_unique}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial_eq_of_eventually}
  \uses{def:fiber_hilbert_function}
  % ENCODING. In Lean the uniqueness is phrased against the chosen value:
  % Scheme.hilbertPolynomial_eq_of_eventually states that ANY polynomial Φ
  % eventually agreeing with hilbertFunction π L F s equals
  % hilbertPolynomial π L F s. Its core is the private helper
  % eq_of_eval_nat_eventually_eq (two rational polynomials agreeing at all
  % large naturals are equal, via Polynomial.eq_of_infinite_eval_eq), which is
  % the statement below. The thin corollary
  % Scheme.hilbertPolynomial_eval_eventually (the defining eventual-agreement
  % property of hilbertPolynomial under the existence hypothesis) is
  % formalized in the same file and carried by this node. All sorry-free in
  % AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/HilbertPolynomial.lean.
  Let \(h_{\mathcal{F},s}\) be the graded Hilbert function of
  \cref{def:fiber_hilbert_function}. If \(\Phi, \Psi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
  both agree with \(h_{\mathcal{F},s}\) at all sufficiently large integers --
  there are \(N_1, N_2 \in \mathbb{N}\) with
  \(\Phi(m) = h_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)\) for all \(m > N_1\) and
  \(\Psi(m) = h_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)\) for all \(m > N_2\) -- then
  \(\Phi = \Psi\). In particular a polynomial agreeing with
  \(h_{\mathcal{F},s}\) for all \(m \gg 0\) is unique when it exists.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \leanok
  The difference \(\Phi - \Psi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) vanishes at every
  integer \(m > \max(N_1, N_2)\), hence at infinitely many points of
  \(\mathbb{Q}\). A nonzero polynomial over a field has at most
  \(\deg(\Phi - \Psi)\) roots, so \(\Phi - \Psi = 0\), that is
  \(\Phi = \Psi\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}\leanok
[Hilbert polynomial from a rational Hilbert function]
  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_of_isRatHilb}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.existsUnique_hilbertPolynomial_of_isRatHilb}
  \uses{def:fiber_hilbert_function, def:ratHilb}
  % ENCODING. Scheme.existsUnique_hilbertPolynomial_of_isRatHilb: from
  % IsRatHilb (fun m => (hilbertFunction π L F s m : ℚ)) d -- the output shape
  % of the graded Hilbert-Serre engine gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational
  % (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) -- an ∃! polynomial eventually agreeing
  % with the graded Hilbert function, with witness Polynomial.hilbertPoly p d
  % via Polynomial.coeff_mul_invOneSubPow_eq_hilbertPoly_eval
  % (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The thin corollaries
  % Scheme.hilbertPolynomial_eval_of_isRatHilb (under IsRatHilb,
  % hilbertPolynomial itself eventually agrees with the graded Hilbert
  % function) and Scheme.hilbertPolynomial_eq_hilbertPoly (under an explicit
  % rational-series presentation, hilbertPolynomial π L F s =
  % Polynomial.hilbertPoly p d) are formalized in the same file and carried by
  % this node. All sorry-free in
  % AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/HilbertPolynomial.lean.
  Let \(h_{\mathcal{F},s}\) be the graded Hilbert function of
  \cref{def:fiber_hilbert_function}, and suppose \(h_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is a
  rational Hilbert function of some order \(d\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}): there
  are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) with
  \(h_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = [X^m]\bigl(p \cdot (1-X)^{-d}\bigr)\) for all
  \(m > N\). Then there exists a unique polynomial
  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) such that
  \(\Phi(m) = h_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)\) for all \(m \gg 0\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib,
    lem:hilbertPolynomial_unique}
  \leanok
  Unfolding \cref{def:ratHilb} supplies \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\),
  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N\) with
  \(h_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = [X^m](p\cdot(1-X)^{-d})\) for all \(m > N\). By
  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
  \(\mathbb{Q}\), applied to the pair \((p, d)\), there are a polynomial
  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N'\) with
  \(h(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-d})\) for all \(n > N'\). Hence
  \(\Phi := h\) satisfies \(\Phi(m) = h_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)\) for all
  \(m > \max(N, N')\), proving existence. Uniqueness is
  \cref{lem:hilbertPolynomial_unique}: any two polynomials agreeing with
  \(h_{\mathcal{F},s}\) at all sufficiently large integers are equal.
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, def:fiber_hilbert_function}
  \source{nitsure-hilbert-quot}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
  % Polynomials" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
  \[
    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
  \]
  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.

  \emph{Status.} The polynomial-extraction half of this theorem is formalized:
  uniqueness (\cref{lem:hilbertPolynomial_unique}) and the extraction of the
  unique polynomial from a rational Hilbert function
  (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_of_isRatHilb},
  \texttt{Scheme.existsUnique\_hilbertPolynomial\_of\_isRatHilb}) are
  sorry-free. The remaining input is the geometric finite-generation theorem
  \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} (Serre), which feeds the rationality
  hypothesis through \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
    lem:hilbertPoly_of_isRatHilb}
  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
  function
  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
  (\cref{def:fiber_hilbert_function}) as a rational Hilbert function of some
  order \(d\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}): there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\),
  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). The extraction lemma
  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_of_isRatHilb} then yields a unique polynomial
  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) with
  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for all \(m \gg 0\),
  as claimed.
\end{proof}

\paragraph{Phase 1: Hilbert polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}).}
On a finite-type morphism \(\pi : X \to S\) with \(S\) noetherian and a
coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) with proper support over \(S\), the per-fiber
Hilbert polynomial is encoded as a function \(s \mapsto
\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). Mathlib provides
\texttt{CategoryTheory.PolynomialModule} and Euler-characteristic
infrastructure on coherent sheaves on noetherian schemes; the polynomial
property of \(m \mapsto \chi(\mathcal{F} \otimes L^{\otimes m})\) requires
Snapper's Lemma, which is a project-side sub-build on top of the
graded-Euler-characteristic infrastructure.
input.new_string:
\paragraph{Phase 1: Hilbert polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}).}
On a locally-of-finite-type morphism \(\pi : X \to S\) with \(S\) locally
noetherian, the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial is encoded as a total function
\(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\), formalized
sorry-free in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/HilbertPolynomial.lean}
(\cref{def:fiber_module_restriction}, \cref{def:fiber_sections_module},
\cref{def:fiber_hilbert_function}, \cref{lem:hilbertPolynomial_unique},
\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_of_isRatHilb}). The \(H^0\)-only route through the
graded Hilbert function of the section graded module avoids Snapper's Lemma
and the Euler-characteristic infrastructure entirely; the outstanding
geometric input is the finite generation \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg},
which feeds \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule} through
\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}.
